literal: possibly from √mā 'to measure, to fashion'; in early Vedic, the creative power by which a god 'measures out' a form; later, the world-fashioning power and (in Advaita) the cosmic principle of apparent manifestation
invariant_definition: There is no genuinely shared invariant. The schools mean substantively different things by *māyā* and the disagreement is not terminological but metaphysical. What is invariant only at the surface: *māyā* is the principle, power, or condition by which the one *brahman* is associated with the appearance of many. But whether this 'association' is illusory (Advaita), a real divine power (Viśiṣṭādvaita and Vaiṣṇava schools), or chiefly a polemical target (Dvaita) is precisely the locus of disagreement. See per-school definitions.

per_school.definition: *Māyā* is the cosmic-side name for *avidyā*: a *bhāvarūpa* (positively existent), *anirvacanīya* (indeterminable as *sat* or *asat*) power, with two functions — *āvaraṇa* (concealment of *brahman*) and *vikṣepa* (projection of the apparent world). *Māyā* and *avidyā* are not strictly distinct in the school but conventionally *māyā* foregrounds the cosmic side (associated with *Īśvara*) and *avidyā* the individual side (associated with the *jīva*). The world is *māyā* in the sense of *vivarta* — apparent transformation that does not modify *brahman*.

*Māyā* is *prakṛti* — real, eternal, not illusory; one of the three tattvas (*cit*, *acit*, *Īśvara*) under *acit*. It is *brahman*'s instrumental power for world-creation; the world it produces is real, not apparent. The Advaita doctrine of *māyā* as *bhāvarūpa-anirvacanīya* is rejected as incoherent (Rāmānuja's *sapta-anupapatti*).

*Māyā* is principally a polemical target. Madhva uses *māyā* in a non-illusionist sense — Viṣṇu's real creative power — but devotes major works (*Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana*, *Mithyātva-Anumāna-Khaṇḍana*) to refuting the Advaita doctrine. Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta* contains the most systematic Dvaita assault on Advaita *māyā*; Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi* is the principal Advaita reply. There is no positive doctrine of *māyā* in the Advaita sense.

*Māyā* is one of Kṛṣṇa's *bahiraṅgā-śaktis* (external powers), itself further divided into *guṇa-māyā* (the material *prakṛti*) and *jīva-māyā* (the power that places the *jīva* under embodiment). It is real, not illusory; but it is *bahiraṅgā* — outside *brahman*'s essential nature — and a *jīva* under *māyā* is in a real condition of estrangement, not an illusory one.

*Māyā* in the Advaita sense is rejected as a Buddhist smuggling (the famous charge: '*pracchanna-bauddha*' — Advaita is crypto-Buddhism). Bhāskara reads *māyā* as Viṣṇu's *śakti* of real *pariṇāma*; the world is a real transformation of *brahman*, not an apparent one.

*Māyā* is Śiva's sovereign *svātantrya-śakti* under one of its self-contractive aspects: the *tattva* by which the unified *prakāśa* assumes the form of differentiated subjects and objects. It is real and is itself a divine power; the contraction is Śiva's free self-veiling, not an alien limitation. *Māyā* here is one of the *kañcukas* (sheaths) of the contracted subject.

translator_note: Translating *māyā* as 'illusion' is the single most damaging convention in English-language Vedānta. It is correct for some Advaitin uses but is exactly what Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, the Vaiṣṇava bhedābheda schools, and Kashmir Śaivism all reject. For most Vedāntins *māyā* is *real divine power*, not 'illusion.' Even in Advaita, *māyā* is not 'illusion' in the modern sense (subjective error) — it is *anirvacanīya*, a positive cosmic principle of indeterminable ontological status, of which subjective error is one local manifestation. The user's thesis explicitly rejects the equation *māyā = illusion* and instead treats *māyā* as the texture of divine *līlā*.
